coredata.dc:title: BSA stabilized copper nanoclusters as a highly sensitive and selective probe for fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Renuka
Majhi, Shukla
Sharma, Keshav
Ali, Mohd
Sharma, Surbhi
Choudhary, Deepika
Tripathi, Chandra Shekhar Pati
Guin, Debanjan
coredata.dc:description: We report on the green synthesis of an effective fluorescent based sensor for selective, sensitive and rapid detection of Fe3+ ions using bovine serum albumin (BSA) stabilized copper nanoclusters. The fluorescence intensity of the BSA-CuNCs has been found to be quenched with the addition of Fe3+ ions. An extremely high selectivity and low detection limit of 10 nM (3σ/k) was observed. The sensor was also employed for the sensing of Fe3+ in wastewater and human blood serum samples. The features exhibited by the fluorescent probe suggest its promising applications in the area of analytical and biological field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
BSA
Fluorescence based sensor
Fe3+ ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of BSA-CuNCs in real samples
Fluorescence based sensing of Fe3+ ions
Determination of iron contents in real samples
Mechanism of fluorescence quenching of BSA-CuNCs by Fe3+ ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: RS thanks UGC, India, and MA thanks CSIR, India, for a fellowship. CSPT and DG thank Department of Science and Technology for the financial support through grant no. DST/WTI/2016/122 and Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi for providing seed grant under IoE scheme (Dev. Scheme No. 6031). The authors also acknowledge help from the CIF, IIT BHU, and MNIT Jaipur for the use of instrumentation facility.
No funding supported our research work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The typical TEM image of BSA-CuNCs (b) The Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of BSA-CuNCs showing the crystalline structure (c) Dynamic light scattering measurement of BSA-CuNCs.
(a) Absorption spectra of BSA, and BSA-Cu NCs. (b) Fluorescence excitation (red curve) and emission (black curve) spectra of BSA-CuNCs solution.

(a) Normalized fluorescence intensity of BSA-CuNCs as a function of time (b) Normalized fluorescence intensity of BSA-CuNCs at different temperatures. (c) Fluorescence spectrum for BSA-CuNCs synthesized at different pHs (d) Relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the pH values at 405 nm.

(a) Fluorescence quenching of BSA-CuNCs after gradual addition of aqueous solution with different amount of Fe3+ ions (b) The plot of emission intensity vs the Fe3+ concentration in the range 0–8 μM. The linear plot of in the range 0.2–2.4 μM is displayed in the inset.

(a) Selectivity of BSA-CuNCs towards sensing of Fe3+ over other metal ions and anions and protein solutions (b) The relative fluorescence intensity of BSA-CuNCs in the presence of 100 M different metal ions, anions and protein solutions and the subsequent treatment with 100 M Fe3+ (red bars).

(a) Normalised spectral overlap between the emission/excitation spectra of BSA-CuNCs (donor) and UV-absorption spectrum of Fe3+ ions (acceptor) (b) Fluorescence decay profile for BSA-CuNCs with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions.
Schematic representation of the synthesis of BSA stabilizes copper nanoclusters.
Schematic representation of fluorescence quenching of BSA-CuNCs in the presence of Fe3+ ions.
Comparison of the analytical performances of the as-prepared Cu NCs with other fluorescent nanomaterials for the detection of Fe3+ ions.
Determination of Fe3+ ions in wastewater sample and human blood serum.

row.entry: Found (mean ± SD, n = 5, μM) (10−6 mol L−1)

head.ce:title: BSA stabilized copper nanoclusters as a highly sensitive and selective probe for fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, Uttar Pradesh, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, Uttar Pradesh, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, Uttar Pradesh, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, Uttar Pradesh, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Rajasthan, Jaipur 302004, Rajasthan, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Rajasthan, Jaipur 302004, Rajasthan, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report on the green synthesis of an effective fluorescent based sensor for selective, sensitive and rapid detection of Fe3+ ions using bovine serum albumin (BSA) stabilized copper nanoclusters. The fluorescence intensity of the BSA-CuNCs has been found to be quenched with the addition of Fe3+ ions. An extremely high selectivity and low detection limit of 10 nM (3σ/k) was observed. The sensor was also employed for the sensing of Fe3+ in wastewater and human blood serum samples. The features exhibited by the fluorescent probe suggest its promising applications in the area of analytical and biological field.

ce:section.ce:para: Ferric ion (Fe3+) exists plentifully on earth and is an extremely important industrial metal. It also plays a key role in many biological processes. Its disorder in the human body can cause damages to kidney, liver and other important organs [1–6]. Natural water, and vegetables are also other sources from which human body takes Fe3+. The permissible limit prescribed by World Health Organization (WHO) in drinking water is 5.4 μM [7]. Hence the sensing of Fe3+ ions is paramount in environment and human health [8–12].

So far, a number of experimental methods have been utilised for the selective and sensitive determination of Fe3 + ions for e.g. inductively coupled plasma-mass-spectrometry, cyclic voltammetry, atomic absorption spectroscopy and optical methods [13–15]. Among all the developed methods, fluorescent based sensing is preferable due to its extremely high sensitivity, and faster response.

In the recent years, metal nanoclusters, which are made up at most of a few tens of atoms have gained tremendous attention of scientific researchers in the areas of sensing, catalysis, environmental monitoring, imaging and biomedical applications owing to excellent fluorescence, low toxicity, high quantum yield, good water-solubility and biocompatibility [16–23]. Researchers have focused mostly on the synthesis, and characterization of metal nanoclusters (mostly gold and silver) for a range of analytical application [24–27]. However, the research on copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) is still limited due to their vulnerability to oxidation and complexities in the synthesis of small particles. The excellent fluorescence, low cost, and earth abundance make CuNCs a potential candidate for a variety of applications, which include bioimaging, sensing, and catalysis [28–34]. Despite these advantages, the extremely low stability and difficulty in controlling the size, which makes them less susceptible to oxidation upon exposure to air, hampers the possibilities of the application of CuNCs [29,35–37]. Therefore the development of chemically stable, monodispersed, photostable and fluoroscent CuNCs in an environmental friendly manner is still a challenge [38–40]. Although, CuNCs have been reported well in the literature for the sensing of many analytes, the application of CuNCs for the quantification of Fe3+ ions is rarely reported.

In the present work, we have utilized an extremely efficient protocol for the synthesis of blue emitting BSA stabilized copper nanoclusters (BSA-CuNCs) at 25 °C. The as-prepared BSA-CuNCs emitted strong blue fluorescence (405 nm) under 320 nm light excitation. The fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs was found to be quenched in the presence of the Fe3+ions. The concentration of Fe3+ present in the sample was determined on the basis of the fluorescence quenching response of BSA-CuNCs. The efficacy of the present method has also been demonstrated in the detection and estimation of Fe3+ in natural water, waste water and human serum samples.

Cu (NO3)2·2H2O and all metal nitrates and chlorides, 30% w/v Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), Sodium hydroxide etc. were purchased from TCI chemicals, India. Bovine serum albumin (BSA), L-Ascorbic acid and concentrated sulfuric acid was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich India. Millipore water (resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm) was used to prepare all the solutions.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements of BSA-CuNCs was carried out using JASCO FT/IR-4700 FT-IR Spectrometer (resolution 0.4 cm−1, number of scans 64). The UV − vis absorption spectra were obtained on a UV − vis spectrophotometer (UV-170, Shimadzu resolution 1 nm). A digital pH meter was used to measure pH values of the aqueous solutions. Fluorescence measurements were performed via spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out using JEOL JEM 2100, operated at 200 kV. TEM sample was prepared followed by the dispersion of nanostructured BSA-CuNCs sample in ethanol solution through mild water bath ultrasonication process, and then the sample was drop-casted onto the carbon-coated copper grid. The dynamic light scattering measurements were performed on Malvern Zetasizer Nano S.
For the detection of Fe3+ ions, 0.2 µM aqueous solution of Fe3+ ions were added into 3 mL of BSA-CuNCs solution. The experiments were performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of BSA-CuNCs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the wastewater sample used was collected from local river. Fresh human blood sample was collected from local hospital. It was pretreated as per the literature reports. [31] A standard addition method was employed to estimate the amount of Fe3+. In order to investigate the recovery studies, in the BSA-CuNCs sample, we added known concentrations of Fe3+ and determined the total iron concentrations at the same experimental condition.
The fluorescence quantum yield was calculated using quinine sulphate into 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference.

The spontaneous aggregation of small NCs in the reduction of metal ions leads to the formation of large nanoparticles. This leads to extreme difficulties in exploring the optical and electronic properties of nanostructured materials [41]. Thus, for the analytical application purposes such as sensing, imaging, biolabeling etc., the stabilization of nanoclusters is an important aspect. Also, to obtain small in size and highly fluorescent metal NCs, the choice of the capping agent is also of extreme importance. In the present work, we utilized BSA to improve the stability of CuNCs without hampering their optical properties. The synthesis procedure of BSA-CuNCs is shown in Scheme 1 . The morphology and size of as prepared BSA-CuNCs were characterized by TEM. The TEM image (Fig. 1 (a)) shows that the CuNCs are spherical in shape, and well separated from each other. The average size of the NCs was estimated to be 3 ± 1 nm, which is consistent with the previous report [42]. The diffraction rings from the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern, as shown in Fig. 1(b), confirms the crystalline nature of as obtained BSA-CuNCs. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements (Fig. 1(c)) shows that the hydrodynamic diameter of BSA-CuNCs was around 5 nm.

The UV visible spectra of BSA and BSA-CuNCs in the wavelength range 200–600 nm are shown in Fig. 2 (a). The pure BSA showed absorption peak at 280 nm [43]. The absence of SPR band at 560 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of BSA-CuNCs confirms the formation of small sized NCs than the aggregated larger size nanoparticles [44,45]. Fluorescence spectrum of BSA-CuNCs exhibited a single peak with maximum intensity at 405 nm, when the excitation wavelength was fixed at 320 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The as synthesized BSA-CuNCs exhibited excitation dependent fluorescence property. The fluorescence measurement was carried out with different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 380 nm. The emission peak was found to be shifted from 400 to 430 nm (Fig. S1†).

The chemical and structural information of the BSA-CuNCs was further obtained from the FTIR spectrum as Shown in the Fig. S2†. The absorption band between 3200 and 3400 cm−1 is assigned to the NH or OH group. The IR peaks at 1650 cm−1, 1365 cm−1, and 1215 cm−1 are associated to amide I (CO stretching vibration), amide II (N–H in-plane bending vibration coupled with CN stretching vibration, and amide III.

we also measured the quantum yields of BSA-CuNCs. The quantum yield (QY) of BSA–CuNCs were found to be about 7.44% before treatment with hydrogen peroxide. After surface etching of BSA stabilized CuNCs with H2O2, QY was found to be 13.23%. In this work, we also investigated the effect of photostability, temperature, and pH on the fluorescence intensity of BSA-CuNCs. The normalized fluorescence intensity of BSA-CuNCs under excitation 320 nm remain unchanged for 120 min, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). This confirms that the as synthesized BSA-CuNCs has extremely good photostability. The fluorescence intensity of BSA-CuNCs was also measured with change in temperature in the range 25–50 °C. Fig. 3(b) shows the plot of normalized fluorescence intensity which confirms that there was no effect of change in temperature [30]. We also investigated the sensing performance of as prepared BSA-CuNCs in the BR buffers from pH-5 to pH-14 using the fluorescence spectroscopy. The isoelectric point of BSA is known to be about 4.7. This leads to the precipitation of the solution with pH value lower than 5 and hence the loss of fluorescence response. Fig. 3(c) shows that the fluorescence intensity of BSA-CuNCs is extremely sensitive towards the pH value of the buffer. The fluorescence intensity exhibited a rapid increase with increase in the pH of the surrounding environment. The fluorescence intensity was found to be maximum at pH value 14 which is more than 3 times than the fluorescence intensity at pH value 5, which is also in agreement with the existing reports [46]. As it can be observed in the Fig. 3(d), the increase in the fluorescence intensity showed a linear response in the pH range 5–14. The linear equation was found to be I = 35.7671*pH + 31.3017 and the corresponding correlation coefficient is about 0.9858. This also indicates the application of BSA-CuNCs for the measurement of pH values in living and environmental samples [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence based sensing of Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The as prepared BSA-CuNCs showed extremely good water solubility, which facilitates their application for the sensing of Fe3+ ions. For this purpose, fluorescence quenching titration experiments were performed with slow addition of aqueous solution of Fe3+ ions. As it can be seen in Fig. 4 (a), the intensity of the fluorescence peak was found to be decreasing with the increasing concentration of Fe3+ ions. The intensity almost quenched to more than 75% for the concentration of 8 µM. This clearly indicates the strong sensitivity of the developed material towards the Fe3+ detection. The plot of emission intensity with the concentration of Fe3+ ions is shown in Fig. 4(b). A good linear relationship in the range 0.2–2.4 µM was observed between the fluorescence intensity of BSA-CuNCs and Fe3+ ions concentration. The linear correlation can be described by the linear regression equation with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9956. The limit of the detection (calculated using the equation 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample, k is the slope of the linear calibration plot) was calculated to be 10 nM from the slope of the linear plot of BSA-CuNCs and Fe3+ ions concentration in the range 0.2–2.4 μM (inset of Fig. 4(b)). A comparison of some fluorescence based sensors developed for the sensing of Fe3+ ions is provided in Table 1 .

To investigate the selectivity of as developed BSA-CuNCs as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ions, we performed experiments involving common metal ions, anions and other small organic impurities. As it is shown in the Fig. 5 (a), the fluorescence intensity was found to be quenched with the addition of 100 μM Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence intensity remained unaffected in the presence of other metal ions, anions and organic impurities. We also conducted experiments to investigate the influence of other ions on the sensing of Fe3+ ions. For this, Fe3+ ions were mixed with other metal ions, anions and organic impurities. In this case, the quenching was found exactly same as that introduced by the blank Fe3+ ions as shown in Fig. 5(b). These results indicate the superior selectivity and high sensitivity of the as developed fluorescent probe for the determination of Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of iron contents in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the utility of the present fluorescent probe, recovery study on the samples with known Fe3+ ions concentration was carried out in the wastewater and human blood serum. To determine the recoveries of Fe3+ ions, standard addition method was applied. The results are shown in Table 2 . In the natural water sample, the recovery of the known amount was in the range 93–103%. In the wastewater sample, the recovery was in the range 95–104 %. In the case of human blood serum, the recovery was found to be 98–104%. The results obtained in this work exhibit the applicability of BSA-CuNCs for the detection of Fe3+ ions in the real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence quenching of BSA-CuNCs by Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching of the fluorophore in the presence of analytes is governed by one of the following mechanisms as reported in the literature: fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), formation of donor acceptor charge transfer complex, static and dynamic quenching mechanism [53]. In the IFE and FRET process of the quenching mechanism, a high overlap between the absorption spectrum of the quencher and the excitation and emission spectrum of the fluorescent probe is observed. In order to understand the exact mechanism of fluorescence quenching of BSA-CuNCs by Fe3+ ions, we investigated the optical properties of both Fe3+ ions and BSA-CuNCs. The plot of spectral overlap between the UV–Vis absorption spectra of Fe3+ ions and the excitation/emission spectra of BSA-CuNCs is displayed in the Fig. 6 (a). As shown in the graph, the absorption of Fe3+ shows a peak around 290 nm. BSA-CuNCs upon excitation with 320 nm, exhibited a strong emission centered at 405 nm. As, a reasonable overlap between the emission spectrum of donor and the absorption spectrum of acceptor is essential for the FRET mechanism to be operative, the results obtained here, as displayed in Fig. 6(a) confirms the nonexistence of the FRET process.

In order to further confirm, we performed time resolved fluorescence lifetime measurement of BSA-CuNCs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence decay curve is displayed in the absence and presence of quencher in Fig. 6(b). As it is clearly noticeable from the Fig. 6(b), the average lifetime of BSA-CuNCs (4.03 ns) doesn’t exhibit any remarkable change with the addition of 4 µM and 7 µM of Fe3+ ions. This completely rules out the FRET mechanism for the quenching of fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs in the presence of Fe3+ ions. As a moderate overlap between the absorption spectra of Fe3+ ions and the excitation spectrum of BSA-CuNCs was observed, the mechanism of IFE is expected to be the responsible factor for the reduction of the emission intensity of BSA-CuNCs in the presence of Fe3+ ions.

In addition, there exist the possibility of other quenching mechanisms such as dynamic (collisional) or static quenching. In order to receive further insight on the possible quenching mechanism, the emission intensity of BSA-CuNCs in the presence of Fe3+ ions was also analysed using Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ
[ Q ]
where F0, and F are the corrected emission intensities of BSA-CuNCs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions, Ksv is the Stern − Volmer constant; [Q] represents the concentration of quencher Kq denotes the quenching constant; and τ is the average lifetime of the fluorescence probe. The plot of F0/F versus concentration of Fe3+ ions is shown in SI Fig. 3. A very good linear relationship with the correlation coefficient 0.9924 was obtained. The slope of the linear plot was used to calculate Ksv and Kq . Ksv found to be 5.57 X 105 M−1 and Kq is calculated to be 1.38 × 1014 M−1 s−1 using the fluorescence lifetime of BSA-CuNCs (4.03 ns) and Ksv . This is found to be much higher than the possible value for dynamic quenching effect (1.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1). Both the high value of quenching rate constant and fluorescence lifetime data confirms the possibility of a static quenching effect [54]. In the present case, an extremely sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions by BSA-CuNCs is due to the strong quenching of fluorescence emission and IFE process (Scheme 2 ) between the sensor and analyte. The results obtained here are also in very well agreement with the recent literature reports [55–57].

In summary, we have reported a modified method of synthesis of water soluble BSA stabilized CuNCs at room temperature having high quantum yield 13.23%. The synthesized BSA stabilized CuNCs are used as a selective, sensitive and rapid fluorescence probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions in water and human serum. The as prepared BSA stabilized CuNCs, under the excitation at 320 nm, showed a fluorescence peak centered at 405 nm and the fluorescence intensity was found to be remarkably quenched with addition of Fe3+ ions. Time resolved fluorescence lifetime decay data were recorded and it was found that the static quenching process is involved between the Fe3 + ions and BSA-CuNCs. The proposed FL based sensor can detect Fe3+ ions with a detection limit of 10 nM. The as prepared materials were also applied for the sensing of Fe3+ ions in waste water, HBS samples and the results obtained confirm its practicability. This work demonstrates that BSA-CuNCs is an efficient, reliable, and feasible sensor for sensing Fe3+ in water and biological samples. The work further illustrates the applicability in the field of environmental remediation and clinical diagnosis.
No funding supported our research work.

DG, and CSPT designed the swork. RS carried out the experimental work with the help of SM, DC, SS and MA. CSPT analyzed the data with the help of RS, KS, and MA. The manuscript was written by DG and CSPT with the help and inputs from all the authors.
Our work does not require ethical assessment as it does not include research on humans and animals.
No permissions were required prior to conducting our research work.

Renuka Singh: Investigation, Data curation. Shukla Majhi: Resources. Keshav Sharma: Investigation. Mohd. Ali: Validation. Surbhi Sharma: Visualization. Deepika Choudhary: Data curation. Chandra Shekhar Pati Tripathi: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Debanjan Guin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: RS thanks UGC, India, and MA thanks CSIR, India, for a fellowship. CSPT and DG thank Department of Science and Technology for the financial support through grant no. DST/WTI/2016/122 and Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi for providing seed grant under IoE scheme (Dev. Scheme No. 6031). The authors also acknowledge help from the CIF, IIT BHU, and MNIT Jaipur for the use of instrumentation facility.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2021.139226.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
